coredata.dc:title: A PEDOT enhanced covalent organic framework (COF) fluorescent probe for in vivo detection and imaging of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhen, Deshuai
Zhang, Shaoqi
Yang, Aofeng
Li, Le
Cai, Qingyun
Grimes, Craig A.
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Simple and accurate in vivo monitoring of Fe3+ is essential for gaining a better understanding of its role in physiological and pathological processes. A novel fluorescent probe was synthesized via in situ solid-state polymerization of 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (PEDOT) in the pore channels of a covalent organic framework (COF). The PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe exhibited an absolute quantum yield (QY) 3 times higher than COF. In the presence of Fe3+ the PEDOT@COF 475 nm fluorescence emission, 365 nm excitation, is quenched within 180 s. Fluorescence quenching is linear with Fe3+ in the concentration range of 0–960 μM, with a detection limit of 0.82 μM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to inner filter effect (IEF), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static quenching (SQE) between PEDOT@COF and Fe3+. A paper strip-based detector was designed to facilitate practical applicability, and the PEDOT@COF probe successfully applied to fluorescence imaging of Fe3+ levels in vivo. This work details a tool of great promise for enabling detailed investigations into the role of Fe3+ in physiological and pathological diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) COF and (B) PEDOT@COF. SEM images of (C) COF and (D) PEDOT@COF. (E) EDS elemental mapping results for PEDOT@COF. (FI) C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p XPS spectra of PEDOT@COF. (J) FT-IR patterns of COF, PEDOT and PEDOT@COF. (K) XRD patterns of COF and PEDOT@COF. (L) N2 sorption isotherms of COF and PEDOT@COF. The inset is the pore width distribution plot of COF and PEDOT@COF. (M) TGA curves of COF, PEDOT and PEDOT@COF. The weight loss before 100 °C is due to water evacuation. (N) UV–vis of COF, PEDOT and PEDOT@COF.

(A) Fluorescent intensities, and (B) the corresponding calibration curves (F0/F) of PEDOT@COF probe towards different Fe3+ concentration (from 0 to 960 μM). Inset photos show the fluorescence of PEDOT@COF before (left) and after (right) adding Fe3+ under a 365 nm UV lamp. (C) Ion-selective study of PEDOT@COF, with all metal ions at a concentration of 750 μM. (D) Recycling ability of PEDOT@COF material for Fe3+ detection.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PEDOT@COF. The inset shows the change in photoluminescence excitation intensity of PEDOT@COF with and without the addition of Fe3+. (B) The fluorescence lifetime decay of COF and PEDOT@COF before and after adding 500 μM Fe3+. (C) Corrected and observed quenched efficiency of PEDOT@COF with the addition of different concentration of Fe3+. (D) The cyclic voltammetry curve, and (E) The Tauc plot of PEDOT@COF. (F) Electron transfer mechanism of PEDOT@COF and Fe3+.

(A) The effect of adding different concentrations of Fe3+ on the UV–vis absorption spectra of PEDOT@COF. (CPEDOT@COF = 0.24 g·L−1). SV curves of PEDOT@COF with different Fe3+ concentrations under (B) 15, (C) 25 and (D) 35 °C.

Photographs under daylight and 365 nm illumination. (A) The University of South China (USC) logo painted on filter paper. (B) Cherry blossom shaped PEDOT@COF test paper strips upon exposure to various Fe3+ concentrations.

Confocal fluorescence images. (A) HeLa cells co-incubated with PEDOT@COF and LysoTracker Red. The fluorescence images of a, b, d and e were collected in the blue channel (λex = 405 nm) and the red channel (λex = 561 nm). c is the merged image of a and b, while f is the merged image of d and e. a, b and c show HeLa cells incubated with PEDOT@COF only. d, e and f show HeLa cells incubated with PEDOT@COF and Fe3+. The scale bar is 50 μm. (B) Zebrafish larvae treated with PEDOT@COF Fe3+ fluorescent probe (λex = 405 nm). The scale bar is 400 μm. (C) Fluorescence images of C57BL/6 mice 1 h after injection of the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe, then at 10, 20 and 30 min after Fe3+ treatment, respectively. (λex = 405 nm). The concentrations of PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe and Fe3+ were 0.24 g·L−1 and 500 μM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of: (A) PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe synthesis, and (B) practical application for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: A PEDOT enhanced covalent organic framework (COF) fluorescent probe for in vivo detection and imaging of Fe3+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple and accurate in vivo monitoring of Fe3+ is essential for gaining a better understanding of its role in physiological and pathological processes. A novel fluorescent probe was synthesized via in situ solid-state polymerization of 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (PEDOT) in the pore channels of a covalent organic framework (COF). The PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe exhibited an absolute quantum yield (QY) 3 times higher than COF. In the presence of Fe3+ the PEDOT@COF 475 nm fluorescence emission, 365 nm excitation, is quenched within 180 s. Fluorescence quenching is linear with Fe3+ in the concentration range of 0–960 μM, with a detection limit of 0.82 μM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to inner filter effect (IEF), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static quenching (SQE) between PEDOT@COF and Fe3+. A paper strip-based detector was designed to facilitate practical applicability, and the PEDOT@COF probe successfully applied to fluorescence imaging of Fe3+ levels in vivo. This work details a tool of great promise for enabling detailed investigations into the role of Fe3+ in physiological and pathological diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Involved in various biological processes such as blood‑oxygen transport, adenosine triphosphate production [1], and trace element metabolism [2], Fe3+ is essential for human health. A Fe3+ deficiency can lead to anemia [3], cerebral thrombosis [4], peptic ulcers [5] and cancer [6]. Conversely, excessive Fe3+ can cause iron toxicity [7], diabetes [8], cirrhosis [9] and neurodegenerative diseases [10]. Further, Fe3+ can be a significant pollutant in industrial wastewaters [11].

Various techniques have been employed for the quantitative detection of Fe3+, such as electrochemical methods [12], ion chromatography [13] and anion-exchange liquid chromatography-inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [14]. However, these relatively expensive techniques are neither simple nor convenient to use precluding widespread application. In comparison, fluorescence techniques are widely used in view of their convenience, high sensitivity, selectivity, and low-cost [15]. Put simply, a fluorescent probe molecule undergoes a significant photophysical change when it binds to the analyte, thereby enabling the detection of different targets [16].

Fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection are of significant ongoing scientific and commercial interest. Ma and co-workers, for example, prepared carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ detection [17], while Al-Azmi and co-workers developed a tricyanofuran hydrazone probe for the identification of Fe3+ in water [18]. However the fluorescent probes reported to date are not suitable for Fe3+ detection and imaging in vivo, suffering from poor stability, low specificity, or high toxicity. Therefore we are motivated to develop a simple and highly sensitive fluorescent probe of stable chemical structure and high biocompatibility for Fe3+ analysis and imaging in vivo.

Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) are highly crystalline porous organic polymers composed of lightweight elements linked by covalent bonds [19], offering high specific surface area, good stability, customizable structure, and tunable function [20]. COFs are being explored for a diversity of applications including gas adsorption [21], molecular adsorption/separation [22], drug transport [23], and sensing [24]. The inherent COF pore structure provides nanoscale-spaces for analyte accommodation, with electron transfer facilitated by the π-stacking structure [25]. A fully conjugated COF material was synthesized by Knoevenagel condensation reaction for Fe3+ detection [26], while a tetraphenylethylene-based COF with a rhomboid-shaped framework was constructed by a Schiff base reaction [27]. However, to date COF Fe3+ fluorescence probes have suffered from low quantum yields, consequently the design of COF fluorescent probes possessing high quantum yields for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ offers significant potential value and relevance in both biological and environmental analysis applications.

As described herein, we have designed a novel PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. As depicted in Scheme 1 , our COF was synthesized by the condensation of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl) benzene (TAPB) with 2,5-dimethoxyterephthalaldehyde (DMTP). Subsequently, PEDOT@COF was obtained through in situ solid-state polymerization (SSP) of 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (PEDOT) inside the homogeneous pore channels of the COF [28–32]. The PEDOT thiophene unit increased the number of allowable COF electron transitions, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. Further, the PEDOT increased the fluorescence efficiency of the COF by enhancing the rigidity of the COF molecular structure. The presence of Fe3+ results in decreased PEDOT@COF fluorescence intensity, which is primarily due to inner filter effect (IEF), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static quenching (SQE).

Looking ahead towards practical implementation of the measurement technique, a simple filter paper strip-based detector for Fe3+ was successfully developed. Confocal imaging studies revealed that PEDOT@COF could be used to monitor Fe3+ in cells, as well as zebrafish. Further, the proposed fluorescent probe enabled real-time in vivo detection of Fe3+ levels in mice.

1,3,5-Tris-(4aminophenyl) benzene (TAPB), 2,5-Dimethoxyterephthalaldehyde (DMTP), acetic acid (CH3CN), ethanol (EtOH), 2,5-dibromo-3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (DBrEDOT), 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), AgCl, CdN2O6·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, PtCl2, VCl3, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, NdN3O9·6H2O and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd.

Acetonitrile (ACN), n-hexane, sodium bisulfite and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) reagents were purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Acetone was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified eagle's (DMEM) cell culture medium was purchased from Labgic Technology Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was supplied by Tianhang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. LysoTracker Red DND-99 was purchased from Epson Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) patterns were recorded using a FEI Talos F200S. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) patterns were recorded using a TESCAN MIRA LMS. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using an Agilent Cary660. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were recorded using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using a Bruker D8 Advance. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a thermal analyzer (TA TGA5500), heated to 1000 °C at 20 °C/min under N2 conditions. Nitrogen (N2) gas adsorption and desorption measurements were carried out using a surface area and pore size analyzer (Micromeritics Tristar II 3020). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550). Zeta potentials were obtained by nanoparticle size potentiometry (Malvern Nano ZS90). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7100). Fluorescence lifetime and the absolute quantum yield (QY) were measured by a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000). Cyclic voltammograms were measured by use of an electrochemical workstation (CorrTest CS350H). Cell and zebrafish images were obtained by confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (Nikon A1+). Fluorescence images of mice were obtained by use of an UVP iBox Scientia 900.
The synthesis of PEDOT@COF is shown in Fig. S1; specifically:

COF fluorescent probe: COF fluorescent probe were prepared according to the literature with some modification [33]. Briefly, an equal quantity of 1,3,5-tris-(4aminophenyl) benzene (TAPB) and 2,5-Dimethoxyterephthalaldehyde (DMTP) was dissolved in CH3CN and sonicated for 30 s. Acetic acid was then injected into the mixed solution while stirring vigorously and kept at room temperature for 24 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed twice with EtOH and dried under vacuum at 60 °C to obtain the COF fluorescent probe.

PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe: PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe was synthesized according to the literature [34]. Briefly, 36 mg of DBrEDOT was dissolved in 1.5 mL of acetone and mixed with 20 mg of the COF. The combination was stirred at 25 °C for 1 h and the acetone was evaporated under reduced pressure (20 kPa, 25 °C). The resulting powder was washed with hexane to remove DBrEDOT from the surface and sealed in nitrogen. The powder, in glass vials, was then heated in an oven at 60 °C for 72 h and 85 °C for 24 h, respectively. Subsequently, the powder was washed with acetone, 1 M aqueous sodium bisulphite and water before being dried overnight at 60 °C under vacuum to obtain the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe material.

A range of solutions with varying concentrations of Fe3+ were prepared and incubated with 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF containing HEPES buffer for purpose of determining the limit of detection (LOD) and quenching mechanism underlying Fe3+ detection. Fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm, λem = 475 nm) were measured after the addition of Fe3+ to each sample. It was investigated the selectivity of PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe by adding 1 mM solutions of Fe3+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pt2+, V3+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Nd3+ to the PEDOT@COF dispersions.

The PEDOT@COF samples were regenerated by treating with EDTA (100 μM) solution for 24 h, with the resulting suspension washed twice using ethanol. After drying under vacuum, the material could be re-used in another adsorption experiment.

The CCK8 assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of PEDOT@COF. First, HeLa cells were cultured in 96-well plates in a DMEM cell culture medium with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 5 % CO2 in a suitable environment at 37 °C for 24 h, with an initial cell density of 5000 cells per well. Following this, the medium was discarded and complete medium containing 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 g·L−1 of PEDOT@COF was added to each well and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, 20 μL of CCK8 was added to each well and incubated continuously at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 0.5 h. Finally, the absorbance of each well was then recorded at 450 nm.

Cell culture was consistent with the above steps. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with one containing 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF and incubated for a further 24 h. The cells were then washed 3 times with PBS solution and then incubated with 2 mL of medium containing 500 μM Fe3+ for a further 4 h. Next, the Hela cells were washed 3 times by PBS solution before re-staining HeLa cells with LysoTracker Red DND-99 for 1 h. After the same washing steps, the fluorescence images of HeLa cells were captured by confocal fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zebrafish toxicity assay and zebrafish imaging
ce:section.ce:para: After obtaining live zebrafish roes from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd., the zebrafish larvae were incubated in different concentrations of PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe solution for 3 days to assess the survival rate. Initially, the hatched zebrafish were cultured in 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF solution for 2 h, then washed with distilled water to remove any residual fluorescent probe outside, and finally immersed in 500 μM Fe3+ solution for 30 min. The fluorescence images were captured by confocal fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemolysis rate testing and mouse imaging
ce:section.ce:para: 450 μL of a 2 % red blood cell solution were combined with 550 μL of PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe solution. A positive control containing 550 μL of water and 450 μL of the 2 % red blood cell solution, as well as a negative control containing 550 μL of 0.1 M PBS and 450 μL of the 2 % red blood cell solution, were also prepared. The samples were then incubated for 1 h before being centrifuged. The optical density (OD) of the supernatant was recorded at 545 nm.

Female C57BL/6 mice (6–8 weeks, ∼20 g) were purchased Hunan Slaughter Kingda Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. Animals were kept under conditions that included light, temperature, free access to water and food. All animal procedures were approved by the Laboratory Animal Ethics Committee of the University of South China (PN: SYXK2020–0002). The PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe was delivered to C57BL/6 mice by a tail intravenous injection, and biodistribution images were captured at 1, 2, 4 and 6 h using a live animal imager. Subsequently, Fe3+ solution was injected into the mice by tail intravenous injection. Subsequently, fluorescence changes in vivo were monitored after 10, 20 and 30 min respectively.

The TEM image in Fig. 1A indicates that the COF is of homogeneous spherical morphology with a visibly rough surface and a size distribution of ≈ 850 nm, which remained consistent after in situ solid-state polymerization (SSP), although dark spots at the interior and more rough edges due to the polymerization of PEDOT in the COF pore are observed (Fig. 1B). The HR-TEM images of Fig. S2 reveal that both COF and PEDOT@COF can be accurately regarded as porous materials [34]. The SEM image in Fig. 1C and D show that the synthesized COF are uniformly distributed and the morphology remains consistent after SSP. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to determine the elemental composition, with C, N and O elements being uniformly distributed in the COF framework (Fig. S3). After SSP, C, N and O elements were still present in the PEDOT@COF, and the presence of sulfur indicated a homogeneous distribution of PEDOT in the channels of COF (Fig. 1E). The trace amount of Br was attributed to the PEDOT.

The XPS spectra of COF (Fig. S4) shows three characteristic peaks in the C 1 s band attributed to CN (284.1 eV), C-C/C=C (285.3 eV), CO (289.0 eV), respectively; the N 1 s band is attributed to CN (398.1 eV), CN (398.8 eV) and NH (401.5 eV); and the O 1 s band at 532.4 eV is assigned to CO [35,36]. The XPS spectra of PEDOT@COF (Fig. 1F-H) shows new characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV and 537.2 eV attributed to CS and C-O-C, respectively. The S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 bands (Fig. 1I) appeared at 167.4 eV and 163.7 eV, which are attributed to the S signal from the PEDOT, and an oxidation peak (S+) is located at 169.6 eV due to the delocalization of electrons from the neutral p-conjugated polymer [37–39]. These results demonstrate successful synthesis of the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. S5 demonstrate the successful synthesis of COF. The appearance of a characteristic peak at 3350 cm−1 for TAPB is attributed to the NH stretching vibration, and the CO stretching vibration of the benzene ring is observed at 1680 cm−1 for DMTP. Notably, the disappearance of two characteristic peaks of both monomers and the appearance of the CN characteristic peak at 1619 cm−1 indicate the condensation of the aldehyde group of the monomer DMTP and the amino group of TAPB via Schiff base reaction, which confirms the successful synthesis of COF [40,41]. Fig. 1J shows the FTIR spectra of PEDOT and PEDOT@COF. The characteristic absorbances at 1358 and 1107 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric CC and inter-ring CC stretching in PEDOT [42,43], respectively. The absorbances at 1070 cm−1 are due to bending vibrations of C-O-C bond in ethylenedioxy group, and the peaks at 976, 629 cm−1 are the characteristic absorbances of C-S-C stretching vibrations in thiophene ring [42,43]. The spectrum of PEDOT@COF displays the characteristic absorbances of both COF and PEDOT, indicating preservation of COF during polymerization. The peaks near 1617, 1353 and 1117 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric CN, CC stretching and inter-ring CC stretching, respectively, indicating PEDOT formation. The XRD pattern, Fig. 1K, displayed characteristic peaks at 2.81 o, 4.87 o, 5.60 o and 7.42 o, corresponding to (110), (200), (210) and (220) COF diffractions planes [43]. In addition, the Pawley refinement of COF yielded XRD profile that are in excellent agreement with the experimentally XRD patterns (Fig. S6), and their difference is negligible. The ultima R
wp and R
p values converged to 9.82 % and 6.49 %, respectively. The relative intensity of the individual reflection peaks decreased after SSP, presumably due to the disordered guest PEDOT polymer in the pores [34].

N2 adsorption and desorption experiments (Fig. 1L) indicate that the isotherm of COF and PEDOT@COF is a type-IV isotherm, which is a characteristic of mesoporous material. The BET surface area of COF is calculated to be 961.62 m2·g−1, whereas the surface area of PEDOT@COF dropped to 61.40 m2·g−1. The pore size distributions of COF and PEDOT@COF, calculated by density functional theory (DFT), are 2.59 nm and 2.23 nm respectively, indicating that the COF pores contain PEDOT (Fig. 1L inset). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Fig. 1M, reveals that the weight loss between 200 °C and 500 °C (≈22 %) is due to PEDOT decomposition, and after 500 °C due to the decomposition of COF [44]. Fig. S7 presents the residue weight percentage of COF and PEDOT@COF samples. In water and the organic solvents examined, COF and PEDOT@COF show less than 0.1 wt% weight loss. Under the ultra-harsh conditions of strong acid (10 M HCl) and strong base (10 M NaOH), the residual weight percentages of COF are 85 and 96 wt%, and those of PEDOT@COF are 90 and 93 wt%, respectively, demonstrating the good chemical stability of both COF and PEDOT@COF.

The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1N) show two absorption peaks from COF at ~258 and ~ 304 nm, the former originating from the π → π* transition of aromatic CC, and the latter being assigned to the n → π* transition of chromophore CN [45]. The broad absorption peak seen for the PEDOT@COF material, extending from 500 nm into the near-infrared range, is attributed to PEDOT [46]. The zeta potential of COF was as low as −10.1 ± 0.07 mV, and increased to −4.1 ± 0.14 mV after PEDOT assembly (Fig. S8), indicating that PEDOT was in situ polymerized on COF. Whereas the potential further increased to −1.0 ± 0.03 mV after the addition of Fe3+, suggesting the potential for strong electrostatic interaction between Fe3+ and PEDOT@COF [47].

Fig. S9A reveals two peaks in the photoluminescence excitation and photoluminescence (PL) spectra at 365 nm and 475 nm, respectively, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 58.97 and 80.59. The PL spectra of PEDOT@COF, Fig. S9B, displays excitation-dependent properties. These are attributed to different surface groups, and hence varying state energy levels [48,49]. The emission spectra of COF and PEDOT@COF are examined under 365 nm illumination. As seen in Fig. S9C, PEDOT@COF exhibits stronger fluorescence intensity than that of COF, which can be attributed to the PEDOT thiophene unit providing an increased number of electron transitions and thus increased fluorescence intensity [50–52].

The absolute Quantum Yield (QY) of COF and PEDOT@COF are measured to be 3.36 % and 9.05 % in Fig. S9D and S9E, respectively, demonstrating that the addition of PEDOT improves the rigidity of the COF molecular structure, increases π electron conjugation, and reduces energy loss from non-radiative transitions for enhanced fluorescence efficiency [53,54].

The fluorescence properties of COF and PEDOT@COF were further evaluated by calculating the radiative and non-radiative attenuation rates based on the following equations:
(1)

=
φ
τ
(2)
k nr
=
1 − φ
τ
where Kr is the radiative decay rate and Knr is the non-radiative decay rate, φ is quantum yield, τ is fluorescent lifetimes (Fig. 3B). The corresponding calculated values of the radiative and non-radiative rate constants are given in Table S1. Compared with COF, PEDOT@COF owns approximately 2.2 times higher radiative decay rate and exhibits lower non-radiative decay rate. This result suggests that PEDOT@COF enables electrons to make more efficient radiative leaps and release more photons. Therefore, PEDOT@COF possesses a higher luminous efficiency.

To ensure the sensitivity of this fluorescent probe, the experimental conditions are optimized. By comparing the fluorescence intensities of the six materials with different mass ratios under 365 nm excitation, it can be concluded that the most prominent fluorescence intensity is observed when the optimal combination ratio of PEDOT and COF is 1:1.8 (Fig. S9F). Subsequently, 0.24 g·L−1 was further identified as the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments by observing the fluorescence intensity of different concentrations of PEDOT@COF at 365 nm (Fig. S9G). To avoid protonation of CN in acidic media, a neutral environment, pH = 7.4, Fig. S9H, was determined to be the most suitable condition for the detection of Fe3+ by the PEDOT@COF probe in the pH range of 6.8 to 8.2. And the ability of the PEDOT@COF probe to combine with Fe3+ in alkaline media results in low detection efficiency due to the formation of precipitates between OH− and Fe3+. [55,56]. It is observed that the F/F0 values, Fig. S9I, rapidly increase within 3 min after the addition of Fe3+ and then remain largely stable. Therefore, 3 min was determined to the optimum reaction time for the next experiments.

Fig. 2A displays the fluorescence spectra of PEDOT@COF as a function Fe3+ concentration. A linear relationship (y = 0.00553x + 1.10488, R2 = 0.997) between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration is observed (Fig. 2B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.82 μM, obtained using the equation LOD = 3σ/S, wherein σ represents the standard deviation for 11 blank sample measurements and S is the slope of the standard curve [57].

Fig. 2C demonstrates PEDOT@COF ion selectivity, using Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pt2+, V3+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Nd3+ ions. The F/F0 values, where F is the fluorescent intensity of the sample with added ions and F0 the fluorescent intensity of PEDOT@COF without any metal ions, shows that fluorescence quenching selectively occurred in the presence of Fe3+. Moreover, even in the presence of multiple competing ions, the PEDOT@COF still has good selectivity for Fe3+. Table S2 shows that the LOD of PEDOT@COF for Fe3+ was of the same order of magnitude, or lower, than other reported fluorescent probes.

As seen in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of PEDOT@COF probe can be regained by adding EDTA to the reaction system, this due to the strong affinity of EDTA for Fe3+ [58–60], thus allowing for Fe3+ to be extracted from PEDOT@COF probe (Fig. S10), with a fluorescence intensity decrease of only ≈ 7 % after seven cycles. Our results indicate that PEDOT@COF is a stable, selective, sensitive, and recyclable fluorescent probe for Fe3+.

General fluorescence quenching mechanisms include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [61], internal filtering effect (IFE) [62], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [63], dynamic quenching (DQE) [64] and static quenching (SQE) [65]. There is an overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the photoluminescence excitation spectra of PEDOT@COF in Fig. 3A, which could be explained by fluorescence quenching due to FRET, IFE or PET. However FRET is non-radiative energy transfer from donor to a nearby acceptor, which leads to the appearance of a new emission band [66,67]. In this study, no new emission bands are generated by the addition of Fe3+, suggesting that the quenching mechanism is not derived from FRET [68]. Fig. 3B depicts the fluorescence lifetime curves for PEDOT@COF with or without Fe3+. The fluorescence lifetime for PEDOT@COF is 6.8 ns, with no significant change to 6.6 ns in the presence of 500 μM Fe3+; the constant PL lifetime again suggests FRET is not the underlying quenching mechanism.

IFE refers to the phenomenon that a target substance absorbs the photoluminescence excitation, and/or that of a fluorescent probe, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the probe [69]. As seen in the inset of Fig. 3A, the photoluminescence excitation intensity of PEDOT@COF decreases after the addition of Fe3+. Therefore, we preliminarily believe that Fe3+ has a competitive adsorption IFE resulting in a decrease in PL intensity. In addition, IFE can be estimated using the following equation [70]:
(3)

where Fobsd is the observed intensity, Fcor is the corrected intensity by removing IFE from Fobsd, Aex and Aem are the absorbances at the excitation wavelength (λex = 365 nm) and the emission wavelength (λem = 475 nm), respectively; s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the cuvette (0.4 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). After removal of IFE from the overall observed inhibition efficiency, the corrected inhibition efficiency of Fe3+ is calculated, as shown in Fig. 3C. The results show that approximately 29 % of the inhibition effect come from IFE.

To verify whether photoinduced electron transfer (PET) is involved in the fluorescence quenching of PEDOT@COF, the highest occupied orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) energy levels of PEDOT@COF are investigated by using cyclic voltammetry. The experimental steps are as follows: 10 μL of 0.1 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF dispersion was added dropwise to the surface of the glassy carbon electrode and allowed to dry. The three-electrode system consists of a prepared working electrode, the Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum electrode, which are placed together in an electrolytic cell filled with a [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution. Subsequently, cyclic voltammetry curves are recorded as shown in Fig. 3D. The HOMO and LUMO of PEDOT@COF can be calculated by the following empirical formulas [71]:
(4)
E LUMO
= − e
E red

(5)

(6)

where E
ox and E
red represent the potential onset of the oxidation and reduction potential, respectively, and E
g is the optical energy band gap. The E
LUMO and E
HOMO of PEDOT@COF are calculated to be −4.66 eV and − 7.03 eV respectively, based on the optical energy band gap value of 2.37 eV obtained from the Tauc plot (Fig. 3E). E
red is found to be 0.26 eV. Furthermore, the E
HOMO and E
LUMO of Fe3+ were reported to be −5.08 eV and − 2.40 eV respectively [72].

Fig. 3F presents the energy level distribution of PEDOT@COF before and after combining to Fe3+, indicating that the excited state electrons of PEDOT@COF are allow to transfer from its LUMO to the HOMO of Fe3+ [73]. Therefore, PET is involved in the fluorescence quenching process of PEDOT@COF.

Fluorescence lifetime decay curves are a reliable method to differentiate between dynamic (DQE) and static quenching (SQE). The fluorescence lifetime of PEDOT@COF is not weakened by Fe3+, see Fig. 3B, suggesting that the quenching process is probably caused by SQE. SQE is a phenomenon in which the fluorescent probe interacts with a quenched molecule to produce a non-luminescent complex, causing the fluorescence to diminish or disappear. This is further confirmed by the different degrees of enhancement and blue shift of the UV–vis absorption intensity of PEDOT@COF at 300 nm and 370 nm with increasing Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 4A). To further verify the SQE mechanism, the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation is applied as an indicator to distinguish between dynamic and static quenching [74,75]:
(7)

= 1 +
K sv
Q

where K
sv is the Stern-Volmer constant, K
q is the constant of DQE rate (maximum value in DQE is about 2 × 1010 L/(mol·s)), τ
0 is the fluorescent lifetimes of the blank PEDOT@COF samples, [Q] represents for the concentration of quencher.

The SV curves of F
0
/F versus different Fe3+ concentrations (from 0 to 1 mM) at 15, 25 and 35 °C are depicted in Fig. 4B, C and D. The SV constant K
sv , which is represented by the slope of the SV curves, decreased from 4.96 × 103 to 2.24 × 103 L/mol as the temperature increased from 15 °C to 35 °C. This indicates that the ligand compounds decompose due to the rise in temperature, in accordance with the SQE. Furthermore, the K
q values at 15, 25 and 35 °C (9 × 1011, 7 × 1011, 5 × 1011 L/(mol·s), respectively) are much higher than the maximum values of DQE (2 × 1010 L/(mol·s)), further confirming SQE characteristics. Subsequently, three possible modes of binding between PEDOT@COF and Fe3+ were postulated (Fig. S11): 1) chelation of unsaturated N and O atoms abundantly present in the COF skeleton with Fe3+; 2) cationic π effect between conjugated π-electrons in the benzene ring and Fe3+; 3) imine-N and thiophene-S donor sites binding to Fe3+ [76–78].

In summary, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of PEDOT@COF for Fe3+ detection originates from the synergistic effect of IFE, PET and SQE. There is no need with IFE, PET and SQE to consider the specific interaction distance between the fluorescent probe and the target, and cumbersome labelling processes are not required [79,80]. Therefore, PEDOT@COF appears a robust platform for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of filter paper strip-based detector
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent scale-use practicality of the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe was explored through filter paper staining experiments. As shown in Fig. 5A, a dispersion of PEDOT@COF was used as an ink to print the University of South China (USC) logo onto filter paper; the USC logo is barely visible in daylight, but emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. These results suggest the potential of the PEDOT@COF for advanced image or data encryption.

To further evaluate the ability of the probe to detect Fe3+ the filter paper, in the shape of cherry blossoms, was soaked in 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF solution for 5 min and then dried. Subsequently, different concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 200, 400, 800 and 1000 μM) were added dropwise to the dried cherry blossom and allowed to react for 3 min. Under 365 nm illumination the fluorescence color of the PEDOT@COF paper gradually changed from bright blue to colorless with increasing Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5B). As such, the PEDOT@COF steeped filter paper-based detector provides the basis for practical Fe3+ detection applications.

The potential of PEDOT@COF for cellular imaging is evaluated by performing HeLa cell cytotoxicity assay (Fig. S12). At the optimal concentration of 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF, good biocompatibility was observed with the cells maintaining a viability of ≈ 90 %. Subsequently, confocal fluorescence microscopy was used to assess the potential of PEDOT@COF for imaging in cells. LysoTracker Red is employed to trace the internalization pathway of the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe (Fig. S13), and the red and blue fluorescence of lysosomes and PEDOT@COF are displayed, respectively. High colocalization of PEDOT@COF and LysoTracker Red DND-99 is observed in HeLa cell, as indicated by a Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.72 (Fig. S14). This indicates that PEDOT@COF could be endocytosed by HeLa cells and enter the lysosomal region, which provides strong evidence for monitoring Fe3+ in cells. As shown in Fig. 6A, red (a) and blue fluorescence (b) was clearly observed when LysoTracker Red and PEDOT@COF were co-treated, with the overlapping channels (c) of the two displaying a consistent result. Upon the introduction of exogenous Fe3+, the red fluorescence remained bright (d), while the blue fluorescence was diminished (e). The results of the channel overlap (a, f) indicate that the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe can be used as an effective imaging reagent to monitor Fe3+ at the cellular level.

The high homology between zebrafish and mammals allows experimental results obtained using zebrafish as a model to provide indirect insight into the biological function of fluorescent probes in higher animals [81]. Therefore, zebrafish serve as an ideal vertebrate model for the use of fluorescent probes in molecular imaging. To investigate the potential of fluorescence imaging in zebrafish larvae, a preliminary assessment of the long-term survivability of zebrafish larvae was conducted. The number of zebrafish larvae varied slightly even when exposed to up to 0.24 g·L−1 PEDOT@COF, suggesting that the PEDOT@COF probe had no effect on the growth of zebrafish larvae (Fig. S15). This was further confirmed by the observation that the zebrafish larvae labelled with the PEDOT@COF showed a strong fluorescent signal in the blue channel (Fig. 6B), indicating the PEDOT@COF was able to successfully penetrate the zebrafish enabling in vivo imaging. Additionally, the fluorescence emission was significantly reduced after the introduction of 500 μM Fe3+ in the culture dishes, indicating that the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe can successfully monitor Fe3+ changes in vivo.

The in vivo fluorescence imaging utility of PEDOT@COF was investigated using a C57BL/6 mouse model. First, a blood compatibility analysis was performed using different PEDOT@COF concentrations. Fig. S16 shows that the five PEDOT@COF concentrations appear colorless or pale yellow, similar to the color observed in the negative control solution (0.1 M PBS solution), while the positive control solution (ultrapure water) appears bright red. The hemolysis rate was only 2.68 % at PEDOT@COF concentrations up to 1.0 g·L−1, indicating that PEDOT@COF probe has good blood compatibility. No acute toxicological abnormalities were observed in the animals during experiments, also indicating that the PEDOT@COF has low toxicity and can potentially be used as biomarkers in vivo.

Biodistribution images were successfully captured by tail vein injection of PEDOT@COF in C57BL/6 mice at 1, 2, 4 and 6 h. As can be seen in Fig. S17, there was a clear fluorescent signal in the distribution of C57BL/6 mice 1 h after injection, indicating that PEDOT@COF probe is able to penetrate smoothly into tissue. At 4 h after injection of the fluorescence signal was still concentrated in the heart, lungs, and kidneys. Notably, the accumulated fluorescence signal of PEDOT@COF probe was still retained at 6 h, demonstrating the feasibility of the developed fluorescent probe for in vivo fluorescence imaging in mice.

Subsequent to Fe3+ injection, the fluorescence intensity of PEDOT@COF-treated mice decreased significantly, as demonstrated in Fig. 6C, indicating that the PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe can detect, in vivo, changes in Fe3+ concentration.

We have developed a novel fluorescent probe, PEDOT@COF, through in situ solid-state polymerization of PEDOT inside the covalent organic framework pore channels. The absolute quantum yield of the prepared fluorescent probe is increased 3 times compared to COF. The PEDOT@COF fluorescent probe exhibits remarkable sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+, with a linear range from 0 to 960 μM and a detection limit of 0.82 μM. The quenching mechanism of PEDOT@COF towards Fe3+ is mainly attributed to the internal filtering effect, photoinduced electron transfer and static quenching. The PEDOT@COF probe proved quite capable of monitoring Fe3+ levels in vivo, and appears a useful tool for detection of Fe3+ in the environment or in living organisms.
